claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit); Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about your child:**

How old is the child you're claiming Child Benefit for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know which age category applies to your child. If you have multiple children you'd like to check, we can go through each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hello! Yes, I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit and I'd like to know if I'm eligible.

I have one child - Alex, who is 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information! Since Alex is 8 years old (under 16), they automatically qualify for the age requirement. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week in paid employment, or not in paid work at all?

*(At 8 years old, I would expect Alex isn't in paid employment, but I need to confirm this for the assessment.)*

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all - he's only 8!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! That makes perfect sense. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

Is Alex **not** receiving any benefits in his own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

*(Again, at 8 years old this would be very unusual, but I need to confirm.)*

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you for confirming. Let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

Is Alex currently undertaking an apprenticeship?

*(At 8 years old, I wouldn't expect so, but just to confirm!)*

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in an apprenticeship - he's just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's exactly what I'd expect for an 8-year-old! Let's move on to check your situation as the claimant.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now let's check your residency status.**

Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements? You only need to meet **one** of these:

1. **Normally live in the UK** - You're ordinarily resident in the UK
2. **Settled status** - You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Pre-settled status and working** - You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3+ months)
4. **Pre-settled status and seeking work** - You have pre-settled status and are actively looking for work
5. **Pre-settled status with sufficient resources** - You have pre-settled status and can support yourself financially (without claiming income-based benefits)
6. **Pre-settled status and studying** - You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. **Pre-settled status as a family member** - You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. **Crown servant abroad** - You're living abroad as a Crown servant

Which of these applies to you? (The most common is simply living in the UK.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you! Living in the UK meets the residency requirement. Let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next, I need to confirm your responsibility for Alex.**

Are you responsible for Alex in any of the following ways? You only need to meet **one**:

1. **Living with Alex** - Alex lives with you
2. **Contributing to Alex's upkeep** - You pay at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards his care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. **Paying maintenance** - You're making maintenance payments for Alex under a court order or agreement

Which applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Wonderful, that confirms your responsibility for Alex. Let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

- **No** - No one else is claiming for Alex
- **Yes, but we've agreed I should claim** - Someone else was claiming, but you've agreed that you will now be the claimant
- **Yes, and there's a dispute** - There's a disagreement about who should claim

Which applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that keeps things straightforward. Let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about Alex's living situation:**

Where does Alex currently live?

- **At home / normal living situation** - Alex lives at home with you under normal circumstances
- **In hospital** - Alex is currently in hospital
- **In care** - Alex is currently in local authority care
- **In foster care** - Alex is being fostered

Which best describes Alex's situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, Alex has been in hospital for a few months now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to hear that Alex has been in hospital. I hope he's getting the care he needs.

Let me check the rules regarding hospital stays.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that. This is important information.

**About hospital stays and Child Benefit:**

Child Benefit can continue for up to **12 weeks** while a child is in hospital. There's also a "linking rule" - if Alex returns to hospital within 28 days of leaving, those stays are added together.

Could you tell me:

1. **How long has Alex been in hospital so far?** (in weeks)
2. **Has this been one continuous stay, or were there breaks in between?** (If there were breaks, were they less than or more than 28 days?)

This will help me determine which rules apply to your situation.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been one continuous stay - about 12 weeks now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. At 12 weeks, Alex is right at the threshold, so we need to check one more thing.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is an important question that could affect your eligibility.

**Since Alex has been in hospital for around 12 weeks:**

Normally, Child Benefit stops after 12 weeks in hospital. However, there's an **exception** - you can continue to receive Child Benefit if you are **regularly spending money on Alex** while he's in hospital.

This could include things like:
- Buying clothes, toys, or books for him
- Providing pocket money
- Paying for treats, snacks, or entertainment
- Contributing to any costs related to his care

Are you regularly spending money on Alex while he's in hospital?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I still buy his clothes, magazines, and favorite food while he's there.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great - this means you meet the exception to the 12-week rule. Let's continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
We're almost there! One final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is Alex's hospital stay being funded through the NHS or your own means, rather than the local council paying for his accommodation or upkeep?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - the NHS is covering his hospital care, not the council.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That confirms everything I need. Let me provide you with the outcome of your assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Good News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit for Alex

Based on the assessment, you meet all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit.

### Summary of Your Situation:
- ✅ **Alex's age**: At 8 years old, he meets the age requirement
- ✅ **Your residency**: You live in the UK (Manchester)
- ✅ **Responsibility**: Alex lives with you
- ✅ **No other claimants**: No one else is claiming for Alex
- ✅ **Hospital stay exception**: Although Alex has been in hospital for around 12 weeks, you continue to regularly spend money on him (clothes, magazines, food), which means you qualify for the exception to the 12-week rule
- ✅ **No council funding**: The NHS is covering his care, not the local council

### Next Steps to Claim:
1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** (or adoption certificate if applicable)
4. **Payments** are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### Important Note:
⚠️ If you or your partner earns over **£60,000** per year, you may be affected by the **High Income Child Benefit Charge**. You can still claim, but some or all of the benefit may need to be repaid through tax.

**Apply here**: [www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

I hope Alex recovers well. Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex (age 8) qualifies for Child Benefit because: 1) He is under 16 years old, meeting the age requirement automatically. 2) He is not in paid employment. 3) He does not receive benefits in his own right. 4) He is not in an apprenticeship. 5) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), meeting residency requirements. 6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as he normally lives with them. 7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. 8) Although Alex has been in hospital for approximately 12 weeks, the claimant qualifies for the exception to the 12-week hospital rule because they are regularly spending money on Alex (clothes, magazines, food). 9) The local council is not paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance - his care is covered by the NHS.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. Despite Alex being in hospital for around 12 weeks, the claimant meets the exception criteria by continuing to regularly spend money on Alex during his hospital stay. All other eligibility requirements are met.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child's cumulative time in hospital or residential accommodation exceeded 12 weeks?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks in hospital/residential accommodation. CUMULATIVE RULE: If child returns within 28 days of leaving, time counts towards 12-week limit. Multiple stays within 28 days are added together.

criteria.calculation_method: Sum all hospital/residential stays. If gap between stays is less than 28 days, count both stays towards cumulative total. If gap is 28+ days, reset counter.
criteria.example: 10 weeks hospital + 15 day gap + 3 weeks hospital = 13 weeks cumulative (exceeds limit)

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Are you regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital/residential accommodation?
response.help_text: Exception to 12-week rule if you regularly spend money on child. For hospital abroad, only if they went abroad for treatment and you're back in UK. Partner's spending counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 8) qualifies for Child Benefit because: 1) He is under 16 years old, meeting the age requirement automatically. 2) He is not in paid employment. 3) He does not receive benefits in his own right. 4) He is not in an apprenticeship. 5) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), meeting residency requirements. 6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as he normally lives with them. 7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. 8) Although Alex has been in hospital for approximately 12 weeks, the claimant qualifies for the exception to the 12-week hospital rule because they are regularly spending money on Alex (clothes, magazines, food). 9) The local council is not paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance - his care is covered by the NHS.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. Despite Alex being in hospital for around 12 weeks, the claimant meets the exception criteria by continuing to regularly spend money on Alex during his hospital stay. All other eligibility requirements are met.
